hints.present indicative: Present would be 'intellegitis' with -i- — 'intellegetis' has the future -e- vowel (3rd conj.)
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'intellegatis' — 'intellegetis' is future indicative
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'intellexistis' — 'intellegetis' is future indicative

hints.lego, legere, legi, lectum: These are the parts of 'legere' (to read) — the base verb without the intel- prefix
hints.eligo, eligere, elegi, electum: These are the parts of 'eligere' (to choose out)
hints.neglego, neglegere, neglexi, neglectum: These are the parts of 'neglegere' (to neglect) — similar compound but different

hints.nominative: 'Ego' is nominative — 'me' is accusative, subject of the infinitive 'vigilare'
hints.ablative: 'Me' can be ablative, but here it is the accusative subject of indirect discourse
hints.dative: 'Mihi' is dative — 'me' is accusative here as subject of the infinitive

steps.prompt: This is an indirect statement (accusative + infinitive). Which verb introduces it?

hints.vigilare: 'Vigilare' is the infinitive within the indirect statement, not the head verb
hints.multo: 'Multo' is an ablative of degree of difference modifying 'acrius'
hints.acrius: 'Acrius' is a comparative adverb meaning 'more keenly'

steps.target: to stay awake / to be vigilant
choices: to stay awake / to be vigilant

hints.to fight: 'To fight' is 'pugnare' — 'vigilare' means to stay awake or be watchful
hints.to see: 'To see' is 'videre' — 'vigilare' means to keep watch
hints.to live: 'To live' is 'vivere' — 'vigilare' means to stay awake

steps.prompt: What tense is the infinitive 'vigilare' in this indirect discourse?
steps.reveal: vigilare = to be watchful (contemporaneous with main verb)

hints.perfect infinitive: Perfect infinitive would be 'vigilavisse' — 'vigilare' is present
hints.future infinitive: Future infinitive would be 'vigilaturum esse' — 'vigilare' is present
hints.present subjunctive: 'Vigilare' is an infinitive form, not subjunctive

hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the parts of 'videre' (to see)
hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the parts of 'vincere' (to conquer)
hints.vivo, vivere, vixi, victum: These are the parts of 'vivere' (to live)

steps.prompt: What case is 'multo' and what function does it serve?

hints.ablative of means: Ablative of means tells 'by what tool' — 'multo' measures how much more keenly
hints.dative of reference: Dative of reference would answer 'for whom' — 'multo' is ablative with a comparative
hints.ablative of manner: Ablative of manner tells 'how' — 'multo' specifically quantifies the comparative degree

steps.prompt: 'Acrius' is the comparative form of which adverb?

hints.acer (sharp): 'Acer' is the adjective — the adverb from which 'acrius' derives is 'acriter'
hints.acre (bitterly): 'Acre' is the neuter nominative adjective — the adverb is 'acriter'
hints.acerbe (harshly): 'Acerbe' is an adverb from 'acerbus' — 'acrius' comes from 'acriter'

translation: Now you will understand that I keep watch much more keenly
